title: Guiding the Thought Step
concept: Forcing a model to write its current hypothesis or goal analysis before selecting a tool, preventing impulsive acting.

exercises.prompt: In the ReAct loop you learned last time, what are the three repeating steps in order?
exercises.explain: ReAct repeats Thought (reason about the state), Action (call a tool), and Observation (read the result). Today we zoom in on making that first Thought step count.

cards.title: Look Before You Leap
cards.body: Imagine an agent that grabs the first tool it sees without thinking. It often picks the wrong one. Forcing it to pause and reason first fixes a lot of mistakes.

cards.title: The Thought Step
cards.body: Guiding the Thought step means making the model write out its current goal or hypothesis before it picks a tool. This planning-in-words happens first, so the action becomes a deliberate choice.

cards.title: Read the Map First
cards.body: A smart traveler checks the map and says their plan out loud before driving off. The agent's written Thought is that spoken plan, keeping it from speeding down the wrong road.

cards.title: Thinking Out Loud
cards.body: Prompt: 'First write Thought: what do I know and what do I need?' The model writes 'Thought: I need today's weather, so I should use the weather tool.' Then it acts. The reasoning came before the tool.

cards.title: Myth: Thinking Slows It Down
cards.body: People think forcing a Thought just wastes time. In truth, a quick written hypothesis prevents wrong tool calls and messy do-overs, so the agent usually finishes faster and more accurately.

cards.title: Impulsive vs. Guided
cards.body: Compare an agent that grabs a tool instantly with one that writes its goal first.

left.text: Jumps straight to a tool and often picks the wrong one.

right.text: Writes its goal or hypothesis first, then chooses the right tool.

exercises.prompt: Why do we force the model to write its Thought before selecting a tool?

options.text: To prevent impulsive acting and make the tool choice deliberate
To make the answer longer for no reason
To skip the Observation step entirely
To let the model avoid using any tools

exercises.explain: Writing the goal or hypothesis first turns a snap decision into a reasoned one, so the agent picks a fitting tool instead of grabbing the first option.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Requiring a written Thought first helps stop the agent from acting impulsively.
exercises.explain: The written Thought forces the model to state its goal before choosing a tool, which curbs impulsive, mismatched actions.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about the purpose of the Thought step.
exercises.sentence: Making the model write its hypothesis first prevents impulsive tool calls.
exercises.explain: The whole point of a guided Thought step is to stop impulsive acting by having the model reason before it selects a tool.

exercises.prompt: Which Thought line reads like a clear, AI-agent style hypothesis written before acting?

options.text: Thought: My goal is to find the user's city, so I should call the location tool next.
idk maybe weather? whatever works lol

exercises.explain: A guided Thought states the goal and names the fitting tool clearly, exactly the deliberate reasoning we want before an Action.

exercises.prompt: An agent keeps calling the wrong tools and redoing work. You want to fix it by improving the Thought step. What change best guides it?

options.text: Require it to write its current goal or hypothesis before every tool choice
Remove the Thought step so it acts faster
Let it pick any tool at random each turn
Skip Observations so it never checks results

exercises.explain: By forcing the model to state its goal or hypothesis first, you turn each Action into a deliberate, reasoned choice instead of an impulsive grab. That single guided Thought step reduces wrong tool calls and wasted retries, making the whole ReAct loop steadier and more accurate.
